title: Teaching
text:
As a teaching assistant, I have developed interactive tools and educational resources for courses in disease ecology and epidemiology at UC Davis. Below are selected materials available for students, educators, or general use.



An interactive simulator for compartmental epidemiological models (SIR, SIRS, SEIR, SEIRS) with density- and frequency-dependent transmission, vaccination, and real-time parameter adjustment.
Features:

Toggle between SIR, SIRS, SEIR, SEIRS, and vaccination models
Adjust R₀, recovery rate, incubation period, and population size
Compare density-dependent vs. frequency-dependent transmission
Visual timeline scrubber and population grid







TipAccess



Access the full simulator here: Disease Dynamics Explorer










An interactive simulator built around the one-compartment pharmacokinetic model with first-order elimination. The tool applies a single governing equation — C(t) = (R_in / kV)(1 − e^{−kΔt}) + C_prev · e^{−kΔt} — to three real-world scenarios, illustrating how the same mathematical framework describes pollutant decay in the environment, CO₂ buildup in indoor spaces, and caffeine metabolism in the human body.
Features:

Pollutant half-life tab: Model exponential decay of a contaminant in an environmental reservoir (e.g., a lake) after input cessation or reduction, with adjustable volume, initial concentration, removal rate k, and new input rate
CO₂ in a classroom tab: Simulate indoor CO₂ accumulation and ventilation washout with editable occupancy schedule blocks, adjustable air-change rate (ACH), per-person CO₂ emission, and a dual-axis chart showing concentration alongside room occupancy
Caffeine in plasma tab: Track plasma caffeine over a 24-hour period by logging individual intake events (espresso, coffee, tea, cola, or custom amounts), with 30-minute absorption windows, adjustable body weight and half-life, and a sleep-disruption threshold reference line at 0.8 mg/L
Real-time summary metrics for each scenario (half-life, peak concentration, steady-state values, time above threshold)
Interactive visualizations with reference and threshold lines







NoteAcknowledgment & Disclaimer



This tool was originally developed by Dr. Scott Bartell and adapted by me for public interaction. It is intended solely for educational and illustrative purposes and does not constitute medical, clinical, or professional health advice. Users should consult a qualified healthcare provider for any questions regarding caffeine intake, medication dosing, or personal health decisions.








TipAccess



Access the full simulator here: One-Compartment Model Explorer










More teaching tools coming soon, including:

R₀ and force of infection interactive exercises

title: Publications
text:
Gordon, S., Pusterla, N., Finno, C.J., Young, A., & Martínez-López, B. (2026). Veterinary Sciences, 13(2), 121.
DOI PubMed PMC






NoteAbstract






ENESFR


This study analyzed data from 63 horses involved in the February 2022 EHM outbreak at the Desert International Horse Park in Thermal, California, to identify host and management risk factors for EHV-1 infection and EHM development. Using multivariable logistic regression and random forest modeling, the study found that older age, participation in jumping (versus hunting), and sharing a barn were the strongest predictors of case status. These findings point to specific demographic and management-related factors that could guide targeted biosecurity and prevention strategies at equine events.


Este estudio analizó datos de 63 caballos involucrados en el brote de mieloencefalopatía por herpesvirus equino (EHM) ocurrido en febrero de 2022 en el Desert International Horse Park en Thermal, California, con el objetivo de identificar factores de riesgo asociados con la infección por EHV-1 y el desarrollo de EHM. Mediante regresión logística multivariable y modelos de bosque aleatorio, se encontró que tener una mayor edad, participar en salto (en comparación con cacería) y compartir establo fueron los predictores más fuertes del estatus de caso. Estos hallazgos señalan factores demográficos y de manejo específicos que podrían orientar estrategias de bioseguridad y prevención dirigidas en eventos equinos.


Cette étude a analysé les données de 63 chevaux impliqués dans l’épidémie de myéloencéphalopathie à herpèsvirus équin (EHM) survenue en février 2022 au Desert International Horse Park à Thermal, en Californie, afin d’identifier les facteurs de risque liés à l’hôte et à la gestion associés à l’infection par l’EHV-1 et au développement de l’EHM. À l’aide de la régression logistique multivariable et de la modélisation par forêt aléatoire, l’étude a révélé que l’âge avancé, la participation au saut d’obstacles (par rapport à la chasse) et le partage d’une écurie étaient les prédicteurs les plus forts du statut de cas. Ces résultats mettent en évidence des facteurs démographiques et de gestion spécifiques qui pourraient orienter des stratégies ciblées de biosécurité et de prévention lors d’événements équins.






Keywords: biosecurity, epidemiology, equine herpesvirus myeloencephalopathy (EHM), equine herpesvirus-1 (EHV-1)




Gordon, S. (2021). Veterinary Evidence, 6(4).
DOI Full Text






NoteAbstract






ENESFR


This knowledge summary evaluated whether soybean oil-based intravenous lipid emulsion (IVLE) therapy is more effective than olive oil-based ClinOleic IVLE therapy for reducing recovery time in cats with permethrin toxicosis. Five case reports and one randomised clinical trial were reviewed. The findings indicate that soybean oil-based IVLE resulted in an average recovery time of 8.5 hours compared to 39 hours for ClinOleic, suggesting soybean-based formulations may be the better option. Standard treatments including dermal decontamination, supportive care, muscle relaxers, and anticonvulsants should be administered based on clinical signs before any intralipid therapy.


Este resumen de conocimiento evaluó si la terapia con emulsión lipídica intravenosa (IVLE) a base de aceite de soya es más eficaz que la terapia IVLE a base de aceite de oliva (ClinOleic) para reducir el tiempo de recuperación en gatos con toxicosis por permetrinas. Se revisaron cinco reportes de caso y un ensayo clínico aleatorizado. Los hallazgos indican que la IVLE a base de aceite de soya resultó en un tiempo promedio de recuperación de 8.5 horas en comparación con 39 horas para ClinOleic, lo que sugiere que las formulaciones a base de soya pueden ser la mejor opción. Los tratamientos estándar, incluyendo descontaminación dérmica, cuidados de soporte, relajantes musculares y anticonvulsivantes, deben administrarse según los signos clínicos antes de cualquier terapia intralipídica.


Ce résumé de connaissances a évalué si la thérapie par émulsion lipidique intraveineuse (IVLE) à base d’huile de soja est plus efficace que la thérapie IVLE à base d’huile d’olive (ClinOleic) pour réduire le temps de récupération chez les chats atteints de toxicose à la perméthrine. Cinq rapports de cas et un essai clinique randomisé ont été examinés. Les résultats indiquent que l’IVLE à base d’huile de soja a entraîné un temps de récupération moyen de 8,5 heures contre 39 heures pour le ClinOleic, ce qui suggère que les formulations à base de soja pourraient être la meilleure option. Les traitements standards, y compris la décontamination cutanée, les soins de soutien, les relaxants musculaires et les anticonvulsivants, doivent être administrés en fonction des signes cliniques avant toute thérapie intralipidique.






Keywords: cats, permethrin, pyrethroids, intralipid, intravenous lipid emulsion therapy, toxicosis

Keywords: cats, permethrin, pyrethroids, intralipid, intravenous lipid emulsion therapy, toxicosis

title: Population Regulation: Stochasticity and Time in Models
text:
A deterministic population does exactly what the parameters tell it to do. You give it a carrying capacity and a growth rate, and it follows that trajectory perfectly. No surprises.
A stochastic population has chance built in. The graph wobbles. It’s messier, but it’s also more realistic, because real populations don’t follow smooth curves.
Here’s the critical point that trips people up: these are two independent axes.




Deterministic
Stochastic




Continuous time
✓ possible
✓ possible


Discrete time
✓ possible
✓ possible



Discrete does not mean stochastic. Continuous does not mean deterministic. You can have any combination of the four.

title: Population Regulation: Stochasticity and Time in Models
text:
A deterministic population does exactly what the parameters tell it to do. You give it a carrying capacity and a growth rate, and it follows that trajectory perfectly. No surprises.
A stochastic population has chance built in. The graph wobbles. It’s messier, but it’s also more realistic, because real populations don’t follow smooth curves.
Here’s the critical point that trips people up: these are two independent axes.




Deterministic
Stochastic




Continuous time
✓ possible
✓ possible


Discrete time
✓ possible
✓ possible



Discrete does not mean stochastic. Continuous does not mean deterministic. You can have any combination of the four.

title: Population Regulation: Stochasticity and Time in Models
text:
Continuous Time Models
Continuous time uses differential equations. Time flows continuously: every microsecond, every infinitesimally small unit. Changes happen smoothly and constantly, and you get smooth curves in your population graphs.
Think of it like calculus: you’re slicing time into infinitesimally small chunks and calculating what happens at each one.

title: Population Regulation: Stochasticity and Time in Models
text:
Discrete Time Models
Discrete time uses difference equations not differential equations, and that distinction matters. Here, time is measured at specific intervals: weekly, monthly, seasonally. Changes occur in steps or jumps, and the graphs look jagged.

title: Population Regulation: Stochasticity and Time in Models
section: Why Populations Overshoot in Discrete Time
text:
Why Populations Overshoot in Discrete Time
In continuous time, a population approaches carrying capacity smoothly. It goes slightly over, adjusts gently, and settles down with nice, smooth oscillations.
In discrete time, the population overshoots dramatically. The organisms don’t “know” they’re near carrying capacity. They produce many offspring all at once (because breeding is seasonal, pulsed), and suddenly the population is way over carrying capacity.
Then the consequences compound: individuals get skinnier, food runs out, fighting increases, more predators show up. The result is a dramatic crash, not a smooth decline. And the recovery is also delayed, creating large oscillations.
The key principle: time lags cause overreaction.

title: Population Regulation: Stochasticity and Time in Models
text:
Converting Between the Two
Here’s something cool: you can make discrete time approximate continuous time by counting more frequently. Instead of once a year, count once a month. Instead of once a month, once a day. The more often you count, the more discrete time looks like continuous time.
This is essentially what calculus does, taking the limit as your time intervals become infinitesimally small.

title: Population Regulation: Stochasticity and Time in Models
text:
Practical Note
Excel doesn’t handle differential equations well, it doesn’t do calculus. So in lab settings, we typically use discrete time models. But remember: discrete and continuous time produce different population dynamics. Your choice of model must match the biology of the organism you’re studying.

These notes are based on lectures from the VME 158 Disease Ecology course at UC Davis led by Dr. Janet Foley and Dr. Sophia Papageorgiou. All factual claims are supported by peer-reviewed literature and official sources (CDC, WHO, OIE).

Influenza: Evolution, Pandemics, and the Road to H5N1



Disease Ecology

Virology

One Health



From the 1918 Spanish flu to the 2025 panzootic: how influenza rewrites its own rules and why we keep getting surprised.



Feb 17, 2026













Population Regulation: Stochasticity and Time in Models



Disease Ecology

Population Dynamics

Modeling



How populations grow, wobble, overshoot, and crash…and why the type of math you choose changes everything.

title: Shadira Gordon, DVM, MPVM
text:
About Me

I am a graduate student researcher working at the Center for Animal Disease Modeling and Surveillance (CADMS) at UC Davis. My work bridges veterinary medicine, data science, and public health through a One Health lens.
My research focuses on disease surveillance, infectious disease modeling, and antimicrobial resistance. I build interactive data tools, including R Shiny dashboards for disease surveillance and livestock health monitoring, that turn complex epidemiological data into actionable insights for researchers, producers, government agencies, and other organizations.
I’m deeply interested in developing strategies for emerging zoonotic threats preparedness, improving global disease surveillance systems, and preventing and controlling infectious diseases through a One Health approach.



Highlights

Toolkit: R Studio, Python, SAS, ArcGIS, QGIS, SQL, Apache Spark, etc.
Research: Observational studies, AMR surveillance, outbreak investigations
Teaching: Disease ecology, infectious disease modeling, epidemiology course content development, etc.

title: Influenza: Evolution, Pandemics, and the Road to H5N1
text: Influenza is, in many ways, the perfect teaching virus. It demonstrates nearly every major concept in disease ecology: antigenic variation, host-pathogen coevolution, zoonotic spillover, and the consequences of public health decision-making under uncertainty. This lecture traces the virus from 1918 to the present H5N1 situation and connects each historical moment to the concepts we’ve covered in class.

title: Influenza: Evolution, Pandemics, and the Road to H5N1
text: Influenza is, in many ways, the perfect teaching virus. It demonstrates nearly every major concept in disease ecology: antigenic variation, host-pathogen coevolution, zoonotic spillover, and the consequences of public health decision-making under uncertainty. This lecture traces the virus from 1918 to the present H5N1 situation and connects each historical moment to the concepts we’ve covered in class.

title: Influenza: Evolution, Pandemics, and the Road to H5N1
text:
Three Mechanisms of Antigenic Change
There are three distinct mechanisms, and we will see the distinction matters because surveillance strategies differ for each.
1. Direct jump: The virus leaps from one host species to another without any intermediate genetic changes. No reassortment, no gradual adaptation.
2. Adaptation: The virus gradually accumulates mutations that allow it to bind to a new host’s receptors. This is slow, stepwise evolution. Think of Michigan’s dairy and poultry worker cases where occupational exposure provided the selective pressure.
3. Genetic reassortment: Two different influenza viruses co-infect the same cell and swap gene segments, creating a novel combination. This is the classic “mixing vessel” scenario.
All three produce antigenic shift. But they’re mechanistically different, and each one creates a different surveillance challenge.

title: Influenza: Evolution, Pandemics, and the Road to H5N1
section: Sialic Acid Receptors: Location Determines Everything
text:
Sialic Acid Receptors: Location Determines Everything
This is one of the most elegant pieces of molecular logic in virology:

Humans have alpha-2,6 sialic acid linkages in the upper respiratory tract (nose, throat)
Birds have alpha-2,3 linkages in the intestinal epithelium (which is why transmission is fecal)
Pigs have both receptor types! This is why they’re called “mixing vessels”

Here’s the part that surprises people: conjunctivitis is the classical H5N1 symptom in humans. Why? Because humans have alpha-2,3 receptors in conjunctive tissue (the eyes). The virus can’t efficiently infect the upper respiratory tract (alpha-2,6 territory), but it can infect the eyes. One of the key insights is that receptor distribution determines transmission route, tissue tropism, and clinical presentation.

title: Influenza: Evolution, Pandemics, and the Road to H5N1
section: The B/Yamagata Extinction: An Accidental Success?
text:
The B/Yamagata Extinction: An Accidental Success?
During the COVID-19 pandemic, influenza cases worldwide dropped dramatically. This was largely attributed to non-pharmacological measures: masking, isolation, behavioral changes that were in place for COVID.
The consequence was extraordinary: one entire influenza lineage B/Yamagata — went extinct. It simply couldn’t sustain transmission under those conditions.
In response, the FDA recommended in 2024 that the annual flu vaccine change from a quadrivalent (4-strain) to a trivalent (3-strain) formulation. The three remaining viruses in the vaccine are A/H3N2, A/H1N1, and B/Victoria.
public health interventions for one pathogen can accidentally (or efficiently?) eliminate another.

title: Influenza: Evolution, Pandemics, and the Road to H5N1
section: 1976 Fort Dix: The Mass Vaccination Debate
text:
1976 Fort Dix: The Mass Vaccination Debate
At Fort Dix in New Jersey, an H1N1 strain resembling the 1918 virus was identified. The CDC director made the decision to pursue mass vaccination of the entire US population.
40 million out of 200 million Americans were vaccinated. However, no pandemic materialized. Instead, many cases of Guillain-Barré syndrome emerged from the vaccine itself. Unfortunately, public health credibility was severely damaged.
The lesson here is about decision-making under uncertainty. With incomplete information and catastrophic downside risk, what do you do?

title: Influenza: Evolution, Pandemics, and the Road to H5N1
section: The Old vs. New H5N1 Scenario
text:
The Old vs. New H5N1 Scenario
This is where the current situation breaks from everything we expected.
The old scenario: Low-pathogenicity avian influenza circulated only in wild birds (the natural reservoir). Occasionally, it would spill into poultry farms, where the high-density environment selected for high-pathogenicity variants. High-path H5N1 was a farm problem, spread through biosecurity failures.
The new scenario: Both low-path and high-path influenza are now circulating in wild birds — the reservoir itself is getting sick. This violates the expected pathogen-reservoir relationship, where we assume pathogens evolve to preserve their reservoir hosts. From wild birds, the virus is spilling over into mammals: seals, sea lions, mink, and — critically — dairy cattle.
Cattle are now acting as a new mixing vessel. HA proteins in cattle-adapted strains are acquiring binding affinity for human alpha-2,6 receptors. This wasn’t in any of the old pandemic preparedness playbooks.
The term for this situation is panzootic — a pandemic in animals — and it’s sadly the stage we’re currently in.

title: Influenza: Evolution, Pandemics, and the Road to H5N1
text:
Where We Are Now (2025–2026)
The current H5N1 clade 2.3.4.4b has done things we didn’t predict: it infects its own reservoir hosts (wild birds dying en masse), it has jumped into multiple mammalian species through independent spillover events, and it’s showing signs of mammalian adaptation through multiple pathways, not just through pigs.
Multiple concepts converge here: the Red Queen hypothesis (continuous coevolution), the amplification effect (high-density farming accelerating evolution), antigenic shift through all three mechanisms, receptor biology determining host range, and One Health as the only framework that makes sense of a virus moving through wild birds, livestock, and humans simultaneously.

title: Influenza: Evolution, Pandemics, and the Road to H5N1
section: These notes are based on lectures from the VME 158 Disease Ecology course at UC Davis led by Dr. Janet Foley and Dr. Sophia Papageorgiou. All factual claims are supported by peer-reviewed literature and official sources (CDC, WHO, OIE).
text:
These notes are based on lectures from the VME 158 Disease Ecology course at UC Davis led by Dr. Janet Foley and Dr. Sophia Papageorgiou. All factual claims are supported by peer-reviewed literature and official sources (CDC, WHO, OIE).

title: Projects

title: Research
text:
I design and deploy R Shiny dashboards for disease surveillance, livestock health monitoring, and epidemiological data analysis. These tools are actively used by researchers, government agencies, and livestock producers.
Click Launch Dashboard to launch any app below for the full experience.



An interactive surveillance platform developed in collaboration with the California Department of Fish and Wildlife (CDFW) and CADMS. This dashboard visualizes spatiotemporal patterns of fish pathogen distribution across California hatcheries, integrating environmental DNA (eDNA) detection data with climatic variables to support early warning and decision-making for aquatic animal health.
Built with: R Shiny, leaflet, ggplot2, plotly
Collaborators: CDFW, Dr. Beatriz Martínez-López



Salmonid Disease Surveillance Dashboard


Launch Full Dashboard




A longitudinal data exploration tool for analyzing subclinical mastitis patterns in dairy sheep at the Hopland Research and Extension Center. The dashboard presents California Mastitis Test (CMT) results, bacteriological culture data, pathogen distributions, and risk factor analyses including parity, lactation stage, and OPP serostatus across multiple sampling periods.
Built with: R Shiny, ggplot2, DT, plotly
Collaborators: Hopland Research and Extension Center, Dr. Beatriz Martínez-López



Subclinical Mastitis Dashboard


Launch Full Dashboard




A flock health and production monitoring platform built for dairy sheep producer discussion groups funded by NESARE. The dashboard tracks ewe milk production, lambing records, and flock health indicators across six farm groups, enabling producers to benchmark performance and identify management opportunities.
Built with: R Shiny, readxl, ggplot2, DT
Collaborators: NESARE, dairy sheep producers



NE-SARE Dashboard


Launch Full Dashboard

title: Research
text:
Research Interests
My research sits at the intersection of veterinary medicine, epidemiology, and public health, guided by a One Health framework. Current and ongoing areas include:


Antimicrobial resistance surveillance in livestock production systems
Small ruminant disease ecology, particularly subclinical mastitis in dairy and meat sheep
Aquatic animal health, including spatiotemporal modeling of salmonid pathogen distribution
Infectious disease modeling using compartmental models (SIR/SEIR), GLMMs, and machine learning
Digital surveillance tools for real-time disease monitoring and risk communication
